patient_profile.communication_style: Speaks in rapid-fire sentences that seem designed to pack information in and move the conversation forward. Does not make eye contact with providers but does with her friend. Answers questions minimally and pivots back to 'I just need to get better and get home.' Laughs nervously when discussing her symptoms. When asked about sexual history, she looks away and gives a rehearsed-sounding 'nothing recent.'
patient_profile.attitude_toward_medical_system: Distrusts it. Fears judgment. Views medical encounters as transactions that happen to her rather than interactions. Wants to be fixed and released, not explored.
patient_profile.key_person: Tanya, her best friend for 18 years. Tanya is in the waiting room and is the one who forced Jessica to come.
patient_profile.key_person_relationship: Tanya is Jessica's rock. They met in high school. Tanya is single with no children and has been Jessica's support during the separation. Tanya intuits that something is wrong and has not left.

reveal_sequence.trigger_detail: Attending notes rash on trunk when performing thorough skin exam
reveal_sequence.information: Patient has maculopapular rash on trunk and face, which she has been covering with makeup. She attributes it to stress.
reveal_sequence.patient_language: I thought it was just a breakout from stress. The separation has been hard. I haven't been sleeping well.
reveal_sequence.emotional_register: Defensive. Trying to rationalize it away.

reveal_sequence.trigger_detail: Attending performs lymph node exam, finds significant cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy
reveal_sequence.information: When palpating, attending finds enlarged lymph nodes and asks about any recent sore throat or URI symptoms beyond what's described
reveal_sequence.patient_language: No, nothing like that. Just felt generally bad, you know? Like everything hurts.
reveal_sequence.emotional_register: Uncomfortable being touched, uncomfortable with the attention.

reveal_sequence.trigger_detail: Standard HIV antibody test returns negative, but attending has high suspicion and orders 4th generation antigen/antibody test
reveal_sequence.information: Fourth generation test is POSITIVE. Attendee also notes mild thrombocytopenia and transaminitis on CBC/CMP.
reveal_sequence.patient_language: That doesn't make sense. I haven't... I mean, that can't be right.
reveal_sequence.emotional_register: Shock. Denial. Realization that this is not 'the flu.'

reveal_sequence.trigger_detail: Attending takes time to explain acute seroconversion illness in non-judgmental way, does not rush
reveal_sequence.information: Patient admits to unprotected sexual encounter 4 weeks ago that she had not disclosed. She is terrified of her sister finding out. She is more terrified that Tanya will judge her.
reveal_sequence.patient_language: It was just one time. It was stupid. I wasn't thinking. I was... I wasn't in a good place. He said he was clean. I know that's dumb. I know it's my fault.
reveal_sequence.emotional_register: Shame, guilt, fear. She is ready to accept blame that is not entirely hers to accept.

reveal_sequence.trigger_detail: Tanya is brought into the room to discuss next steps
reveal_sequence.information: Tanya immediately says 'I knew something was wrong' and holds Jessica's hand. She is not judgmental. She is furious on Jessica's behalf at the situation.
reveal_sequence.patient_language: Tanya: 'We're going to get you help. You're not alone in this.' Jessica: (crying) 'I have to tell my sister. I have to figure this out with Maya.'
reveal_sequence.emotional_register: Jessica: relieved, terrified, sad. Tanya: protective, angry at the virus more than at Jessica.

outcome_trajectory.correct_treatment: Immediate confirmation with HIV RNA PCR (already done), CD4 count, resistance testing. Start antiretroviral therapy immediately (integrase inhibitor-based regimen most appropriate for acute infection). Infectious disease consult. Case management for support and disclosure planning. Partner notification and testing. Tanya gets tested. Counseling and support groups. Plans for Maya's care and telling father appropriately.
outcome_trajectory.correct_outcome: Jessica is connected to ID care and starts treatment within 24 hours. CD4 count is measured at 580 (relatively preserved because this is acute infection). Viral load suppresses to undetectable on therapy. She remains healthy. She discloses to her sister, who is initially shocked but ultimately supportive. She tells Maya's father in controlled way with support from her social worker. She does not lose custody. She becomes undetectable=untransmittable and lives a long healthy life. This changes her trajectory but does not destroy it.
outcome_trajectory.missed_diagnosis: If attending relies on negative standard antibody test and discharges with 'probable flu' and Tamiflu: Jessica continues spreading virus through unknowing sexual contact. Her viral load remains extremely high (180k copies). She likely has another sexual encounter within the next week or two because she thinks she has the flu and will recover. She progresses to symptomatic AIDS in 6-12 months if untreated. She returns to ED much sicker.
outcome_trajectory.resident_catches_it_unsupervised: A resident thinks 'fever, rash, lymphadenopathy, mild transaminitis' and puts acute viral syndrome into the differential. Orders fourth-generation HIV test as part of workup for fever of unknown origin. Test is positive. Resident does not panic, does not shame the patient, immediately calls ID and infectious disease comes down to help with first-line treatment. Patient is treated appropriately immediately.
outcome_trajectory.resident_misses_it_unsupervised: A resident anchors on 'flu season' and 'negative HIV antibody test means not HIV.' Patient discharged with Tamiflu and 'rest, fluids, fever management.' No ID involvement. Jessica takes Tamiflu (which is ineffective for HIV), continues to feel terrible in a different way after 48 hours, but assumes it is because the flu is 'worse than usual.' She returns in 4 weeks septic with opportunistic infection.
outcome_trajectory.follow_up_hook: Jessica's disclosure to her family and partner will be a major ongoing social and emotional process. Her connection to ID care and public health will be long-term. There is potential for her to become a mentor to other newly diagnosed patients at Riverside General. Her case will also highlight the need for careful fourth-generation testing in the ED.

systemic_flags.world_event_connection: Jessica's vulnerability to this infection stems partly from the economic stress of Riverside—she is overworked, underpaid, dealing with housing instability and a single-parent household, which made her emotionally vulnerable during the encounter. This is the same ecosystem that produced Marcus Chen and Patricia DeMarco.
systemic_flags.future_seed: Jessica's regular ID appointments at Riverside General will make her a known patient. If she successfully suppresses her viral load and becomes undetectable, she may become a peer educator for the hospital's community education program in a year or two. She may also be a resource if other young single mothers come in with similar presentations.

cases.narrative_hook: A 6-year-old was brought in by his mother because he tripped on the stairs at home this afternoon and hit his head. He seems fine—he's alert, talking, not complaining much. The mother is reasonably calm. He did not lose consciousness. No vomiting. Just a small bump on his forehead.

presenting_layer.chief_complaint: Head trauma from fall, minor mechanism
presenting_layer.triage_note: 6-year-old male with head injury from fall at home. Alert, playful, GCS 15. Small laceration over right forehead. Mother reports no LOC, no vomiting, normal behavior since incident. Vital signs reassuring.

medical_truth.true_diagnosis: Minor head injury with small scalp laceration. Concussion risk but low probability of intracranial injury given mechanism and clinical presentation. No acute neurosurgical intervention needed.
supporting_findings: Age 6, mechanism is low-velocity fall from stairs (approximately 4-5 feet)
Alert and playful in ED, GCS 15
Small laceration over right forehead, approximately 1 cm, no active bleeding
No focal neurologic deficits on brief exam
No vomiting or severe headache
Mother reports normal mental status since injury
Normal vital signs for age
medical_truth.what_labs_show: No labs needed for this patient. Routine labs would be normal.
medical_truth.what_imaging_shows: Head CT (if ordered) would be normal. No intracranial hemorrhage, no skull fracture, no edema. CT is not indicated by Ottawa Head Injury Rules for pediatric patients.
medical_truth.classic_miss_reason: The classic miss with minor pediatric head injuries is overtesting—ordering CT scans and labs when they are not indicated, causing unnecessary radiation exposure and cost, and creating a sick role for a healthy child. This is actually a case where the miss is in doing too much, not too little. Good practice is reassurance, wound care, clear return precautions, and discharge.

patient_profile.living_situation: Lives with mother (healthcare worker at a different hospital), father, and 3-year-old sister in a house in Riverside neighborhood
patient_profile.why_they_came_today: His mother saw him fall and immediately wanted to rule out anything serious. She works in healthcare (nursing) and knows that head injuries require evaluation. She is being appropriately cautious.
patient_profile.what_theyre_not_saying: Nothing medically significant. She is not hiding anything. She is being a good parent.
patient_profile.what_they_fear: That he has a serious head injury that is not obvious. That she will miss something. That he will have permanent brain damage. These are normal parental fears.
patient_profile.what_they_are_protecting: Her peace of mind. The sense that her children are safe.
patient_profile.communication_style: Clear, coherent, detailed history. Answers questions directly. Asks appropriate questions. Observant about her son's behavior. Not anxious in a pathologic way—appropriately concerned.
patient_profile.attitude_toward_medical_system: Trusting and respectful. Understands medical procedures. Does not demand unnecessary testing but does want reassurance.
patient_profile.key_person: His mother, Sarah. Present and engaged.
patient_profile.key_person_relationship: Normal, healthy parent-child relationship. She is his advocate and protector.

reveal_sequence.trigger_detail: Provider performs careful neurologic exam including cranial nerves, motor function, cerebellar testing
reveal_sequence.information: All normal. No focal deficits. Child is playful and cooperative during exam.
reveal_sequence.patient_language: David: 'Can I go home now?' Mother: 'They just want to make sure you're okay, buddy.'
reveal_sequence.emotional_register: David: impatient, not distressed. Mother: relieved as exam progresses.

reveal_sequence.trigger_detail: Provider asks David about the fall and what happened right after
reveal_sequence.information: David accurately describes tripping on stairs and hitting his head on the edge of a step. Immediately got up and cried. Mother was there within seconds.
reveal_sequence.patient_language: David: 'I was running down the stairs and my foot got caught. I hit my head really hard and it hurt. Mom came and hugged me.'
reveal_sequence.emotional_register: Matter-of-fact. He is genuinely okay emotionally.

outcome_trajectory.correct_treatment: Wound closure (small laceration can be closed with tissue adhesive or small sutures), wound care instructions. Clear discharge instructions regarding return precautions: return if develops severe headache, repeated vomiting, altered consciousness, difficulty speaking, weakness, or behavioral change. Reassurance to mother. No imaging needed. No labs needed.
outcome_trajectory.correct_outcome: David is discharged home with his mother. Laceration heals without complication. He experiences minor post-concussive symptoms (slight headache, mild attention in school for 2-3 days). Mother monitors him closely per instructions. No return visit needed. Child recovers fully.
outcome_trajectory.missed_diagnosis: There is no diagnosis to miss. This is a normal, appropriate case of minor head injury that does not require intervention. The risk is iatrogenic harm from overtesting.
outcome_trajectory.resident_catches_it_unsupervised: A resident sees 'child, head injury' and briefly thinks 'CT scan,' but then applies Ottawa Head Injury Rules, which are validated for exactly this age and mechanism. No criteria are met. Resident performs good neuro exam, documents findings, discusses with attending or senior resident, confirms no imaging needed. Appropriate discharge.
outcome_trajectory.resident_misses_it_unsupervised: A resident, uncertain about whether to image, orders a CT 'just to be safe.' CT is normal (as expected). Child is unnecessarily exposed to radiation. Family receives a $4,000 bill for an unnecessary test. Parents become anxious because 'they wouldn't have ordered it if they weren't worried.' This is bad practice and common in pediatric head injury.
outcome_trajectory.follow_up_hook: None. This is a straightforward case with a good outcome. It serves as a contrast to the more complex cases on the shift. The value of this case is in teaching appropriate use of imaging and resistance to defensive medicine.

cases.narrative_hook: A woman whose sister drove in from out of state with a cough that won't stop, and the sister keeps checking her phone like she's waiting for someone to call and tell her she made a mistake coming here.

presenting_layer.chief_complaint: Cough for 10 days, fever, shortness of breath
presenting_layer.triage_note: Productive cough, fever 101.8, O2 sat 92 on room air. Reports started Sunday evening. Sister at bedside. Lives in [another state].

medical_truth.true_diagnosis: Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) with early sepsis, likely bacterial superimposed on influenza-like illness
supporting_findings: Fever 101.8 with productive cough and progressive dyspnea
Hypoxia 92% on room air
Tachycardia 108
Tachypnea 22
Elevated BP likely compensatory
Reports myalgias and malaise for first 3-4 days, then localized to chest
Denies significant past medical history but admits she 'never goes to doctors'
Cough productive of green sputum for past 3 days
medical_truth.what_labs_show: CBC shows WBC 14.2 with left shift. CMP normal. Lactate 2.1 (lower end of elevated). Blood cultures pending. Procalcitonin 0.8 (consistent with bacterial infection).
medical_truth.what_imaging_shows: Chest X-ray shows right lower lobe infiltrate, no effusion, no pneumothorax.
red_herrings: Sister keeps mentioning 'everyone at work is sick' — encourages assumption of simple viral URI that will self-resolve
Patient is relatively upright and speaking in sentences — can minimize severity of hypoxia
Early in sepsis course — no hypotension yet, no altered mental status
Works in a school setting — suggests viral etiology to both patient and residents
medical_truth.classic_miss_reason: The patient herself is downplaying this. She came in because her sister insisted ('You sound terrible on the phone, I'm driving over'), not because she thought it was serious. The sister is also somewhat dismissive — 'everyone has this thing going around.' A resident can get pulled into that narrative, especially on a busy evening shift. The O2 sat of 92 is easy to rationalize as 'not that bad,' and the patient will agree. The presentation reads as 'bronchitis, probably viral' unless the clinician actively treats the hypoxia and fever together as an urgent problem. The early sepsis findings are subtle. A history-and-antibiotics-go-home approach would be the classic miss.

patient_profile.living_situation: Lives alone in [another state], three hours from sister
patient_profile.why_they_came_today: Her sister called Tuesday night and heard her coughing through the phone. Insisted on driving over Wednesday afternoon and brought her directly to the ED Thursday evening because Margaret's fever spiked to 102 and she woke up short of breath.
patient_profile.what_theyre_not_saying: Margaret has avoided the medical system deliberately for ten years — not antivaccine, just hasn't prioritized it and doesn't trust it much. She's been managing her life fine without it. She's also embarrassed that her sister had to drive three hours to take care of her. There's a guilt operating underneath her downplaying of symptoms.
patient_profile.what_they_fear: That this will spiral into something that requires her to miss work (school year is in session and she has no substitute plans). That she'll have to admit to her principal that she should have stayed home longer. That her sister will tell their mother, which will trigger a whole family narrative about how Margaret 'doesn't take care of herself.'
patient_profile.what_they_are_protecting: Her independence and her competence. She is a person who handles things. Getting seriously ill is admitting she is not handling something, and that matters to her more than being honest about how bad she feels.
patient_profile.communication_style: Answers questions directly but trails off at the end, as if deciding mid-sentence whether to finish the thought. Makes eye contact but keeps checking her sister's face, as if looking for permission to be taking this seriously. Laughs slightly when mentioning her symptoms, a habit that undercuts their gravity.
patient_profile.attitude_toward_medical_system: Skeptical and mildly defensive. No major trauma history, just a general sense that she doesn't fit into medical spaces and that doctors overtreat things. Came in reluctantly and would prefer to leave soon.
patient_profile.key_person: Her sister Rachel, who drove three hours to get her and is now sitting in the room looking simultaneously worried and like she's already doing mental math about how long she can stay before she needs to drive back.
patient_profile.key_person_relationship: Close enough that Rachel noticed something was wrong over the phone. Not close enough that Margaret trusts Rachel won't tell their mother everything. This drives a lot of Margaret's behavior.

reveal_sequence.trigger_detail: When asked how long she's had the green sputum production, Margaret hesitates
reveal_sequence.information: It's been three days, not 'just a little bit.' She's been coughing up mucus most of the day when she wakes up.
reveal_sequence.patient_language: Yeah, like... since Tuesday morning I guess. Every morning it's there. I thought it would clear up but it didn't.
reveal_sequence.emotional_register: Slightly ashamed, as if admitting the symptom is worse than experiencing it

reveal_sequence.trigger_detail: When the attending auscultates the right lower lobe and finds crackles and decreased air movement
reveal_sequence.information: This is infiltrate, not bronchitis. The exam finding makes the CXR finding suddenly matter.
reveal_sequence.patient_language: Margaret doesn't say anything, but her sister leans forward slightly.
reveal_sequence.emotional_register: The clinical finding shifts the room's emotional temperature. Reality check.

reveal_sequence.trigger_detail: When the attending returns with the CXR images and shows her the right lower lobe infiltrate
reveal_sequence.information: Margaret sees it and something in her face changes. The sister says 'see, that's why I drove over.' Margaret's eyes get wet slightly.
reveal_sequence.patient_language: Oh. I didn't... I thought it was just a cold. I thought it would go away.
reveal_sequence.emotional_register: A moment of fear passing across her face, followed by relief that someone else is now responsible for this

reveal_sequence.trigger_detail: After admission decision is made and the attending explains the need for antibiotics and likely 2-3 day admission
reveal_sequence.information: Margaret mentions she hasn't had a physical exam in nine years. Her sister touches her arm. Margaret is now actually listening instead of waiting to leave.
reveal_sequence.patient_language: I know I should probably... I've just been busy. And I don't really have a regular doctor.
reveal_sequence.emotional_register: Vulnerable. The defenses are down slightly now that someone else has taken the problem seriously.

outcome_trajectory.correct_treatment: Admit for IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + azithromycin pending cultures, but empiric coverage started immediately). Supplemental oxygen to maintain O2 sat >94%. IV fluids. Close monitoring for sepsis progression. Repeat vitals in 2 hours.
outcome_trajectory.correct_outcome: Responds well to antibiotics. By hospital day 2, fever down, O2 sat improves to 95% on room air, productive cough begins to resolve. Discharged on day 3 with oral antibiotics and close outpatient follow-up. Sister stays overnight, takes her home, helps her arrange for substitute coverage at school. Uses the hospitalization as a pivot point to establish primary care.
outcome_trajectory.missed_diagnosis: If the clinician treats this as viral URI and sends her home with supportive care and 'call if it gets worse' — patient gets worse at home over the next 36 hours, develops hypotension and altered mental status, comes back in ambulance with full sepsis. Much harder recovery, longer ICU stay, potential for multi-organ failure.
outcome_trajectory.resident_catches_it_unsupervised: Resident notes the combination of fever + hypoxia + infiltrate on CXR, initiates antibiotics promptly, calls for admission. Attending approves. Clean pathway.
outcome_trajectory.resident_misses_it_unsupervised: Resident frames this as 'viral respiratory illness, patient is stable enough to go home with follow-up.' Doesn't weight the hypoxia heavily because patient is not in distress. Doesn't connect fever + infiltrate urgently. Patient discharged.
outcome_trajectory.follow_up_hook: Margaret establishes primary care with a physician at Riverside General's outpatient clinic. This becomes her anchor to the healthcare system. A resident who admits her well might see her again in six weeks for a check-up.

systemic_flags.world_event_connection: Early flu season + respiratory illness uptick in the community. Margaret works in a school (one of the major transmission points in a community). Her case is part of a broader wave starting to hit Riverside, but it's still early enough that not every clinician will recognize it as urgent.
systemic_flags.future_seed: If this patient is admitted and treated well, she becomes an anchor case for the week. If she's missed and comes back in septic shock, she becomes a teaching case about cognitive bias and downplaying patient presentation.

cases.narrative_hook: A man who came in because he tripped on the sidewalk outside the building, and he's annoyed about the inconvenience, and his friend who drove him is acting like this is a bigger deal than it actually is.

presenting_layer.chief_complaint: Fall, left ankle swelling, mild head pain
presenting_layer.triage_note: Slipped on wet concrete outside ED entrance approximately 20 minutes ago. Denies loss of consciousness. Left ankle swollen, able to bear weight. Small laceration on forehead, no active bleeding. Alert and oriented. Friend accompanying.

medical_truth.true_diagnosis: Uncomplicated ankle sprain (Grade I), simple laceration to forehead. However, BP 142/88 is elevated for this patient's baseline — indicates he likely has undiagnosed hypertension.
supporting_findings: Ankle swelling localized to lateral malleolus region
Can bear weight, though with discomfort
ROM limited in inversion/eversion but intact
Laceration is superficial, 0.5 cm, not gaping
No neuro deficits
GCS 15, alert and oriented
Vitals otherwise stable
medical_truth.what_labs_show: No labs indicated for this presentation.
medical_truth.what_imaging_shows: Ankle X-rays (if obtained) would likely show no fracture. The clinical presentation does not warrant imaging in the first instance.
red_herrings: The elevated BP in the setting of acute pain from a fall — easy to attribute to pain response rather than recognizing it as baseline hypertension
The 'convenient' presentation of a fall as the only reason for the visit — encourages anchoring on the ankle/laceration and missing the BP entirely
The patient himself is irritated about being here and dismissive of his symptoms — signals 'routine case' to the clinician
medical_truth.classic_miss_reason: The clinician focuses on the injury (ankle + laceration) because that's the presenting complaint and it's straightforward. The BP is noted, filed, and not explored. In a routine evening shift with throughput pressure, there's no trigger to ask about hypertension history, symptoms of cardiovascular disease, or baseline BP. The patient is not in crisis. His vitals are 'normal' for someone who just had a painful injury. A quick discharge with ankle care instructions and suture closure happens without the missed opportunity to identify uncontrolled hypertension that this patient likely doesn't know he has.

patient_profile.living_situation: Lives alone in a studio apartment; divorced, no kids, relatively isolated socially
patient_profile.why_they_came_today: He tripped on wet concrete in front of the ED because his friend Marcus insisted on driving him here after the fall. David wanted to go home and ice his ankle. Marcus worked in healthcare briefly years ago and gets cautious about head injuries.
patient_profile.what_theyre_not_saying: David hasn't been to a doctor in over fifteen years. Not because he's avoidant exactly, but because he's been healthy enough and work has been consuming and he just never made it happen. He has no idea his blood pressure is this high. He would be shocked to know he has hypertension.
patient_profile.what_they_fear: Being told he needs to take medication, go to follow-up appointments, change his life somehow. He's fifty-eight, still working full-time at a demanding job, and adding medical complexity to that feels like a burden he can't take on right now.
patient_profile.what_they_are_protecting: His sense of self as a person who is fine. Who doesn't need help. Who can handle what he's got. His independence is tied up in not being the kind of person who has medical problems.
patient_profile.communication_style: Speaks in short sentences with a slightly dismissive tone. When asked questions about his health history, he gives minimal answers. 'Never had any problems.' 'Don't take any medications.' 'Feel fine most of the time.' His answers are complete sentences but feel like they're closing conversations rather than opening them. He looks at the clock twice.
patient_profile.attitude_toward_medical_system: Mild contempt mixed with indifference. Believes that if something were really wrong, he would feel worse. Sees healthcare as something for people who are actually sick, not for minor injuries.
patient_profile.key_person: Marcus, his friend and coworker of eighteen years, who is genuinely worried about him, has noticed he seems more winded lately, and drove him here despite David's protests. Marcus is sitting in the waiting room and texted him 'get them to check you out properly' while David was walking in.
patient_profile.key_person_relationship: Marcus is his closest friend and probably his lifeline to social connection. David doesn't fully realize how much he depends on Marcus's judgment. But he also resists it sometimes because accepting Marcus's concern means accepting that something might actually be wrong.

reveal_sequence.trigger_detail: When the attending asks about past medical history and David says 'never had any problems,' the attending follows up: 'When's the last time you had your blood pressure checked?'
reveal_sequence.information: David pauses. He doesn't actually know. It's been at least fifteen years. Probably longer.
reveal_sequence.patient_language: I don't know. High school? Maybe college? I've never had any issues with it or anything.
reveal_sequence.emotional_register: Slightly defensive, but also a moment of genuine uncertainty about his own body

reveal_sequence.trigger_detail: The attending retakes BP manually after the initial reading, and gets 144/86. The attending notes it and says it's elevated.
reveal_sequence.information: David learns for the first time that he has high blood pressure. This is not dramatic, but it is new information that he didn't have before.
reveal_sequence.patient_language: Well... I mean, I just fell. I'm probably just tense. Is that bad?
reveal_sequence.emotional_register: A small moment of worry passes over his face, followed by rationalization

reveal_sequence.trigger_detail: After the ankle is cleared and the laceration is closed, the attending sits down and explains that elevated BP is common, treatable, but needs follow-up and monitoring.
reveal_sequence.information: David hears that this is real and will require him to do something about it. He also hears that it's not immediately dangerous but shouldn't be ignored.
reveal_sequence.patient_language: So like... what do I do? Do I need medication? Because I'm not really... I don't really want to take medication if I don't have to.
reveal_sequence.emotional_register: Worry mixed with resistance. He's hearing the message but also trying to negotiate out of it.

reveal_sequence.trigger_detail: The attending suggests David schedule a follow-up appointment with a primary care provider, and mentions that Marcus is in the waiting room. David brings Marcus back in, and the attending repeats the BP concern to both of them.
reveal_sequence.information: Marcus hears about the BP and nods like 'I knew something was going on.' David sees his friend's concern validated by the doctor. A small shift happens in David's resistance.
reveal_sequence.patient_language: Marcus: 'I told you you were getting winded.' David: 'I'm fine. It's just... a lot of stairs.' Marcus to the doctor: 'He refuses to slow down.' Doctor: 'He needs to get this checked.'
reveal_sequence.emotional_register: David feels a mix of irritation at being called out and relief that someone else sees it and cares

outcome_trajectory.correct_treatment: Ankle: RICE protocol, ankle wrap/brace, pain management as needed. Laceration: cleaned, closed with steri-strips or sutures. Blood pressure: documented, discussed. Patient given referral to primary care physician at Riverside General's outpatient clinic. Advised to follow up within 1-2 weeks for BP recheck and establishment of care. Educated on hypertension risk factors.
outcome_trajectory.correct_outcome: David follows up with primary care (Marcus helps him make the appointment and reminds him to go). Primary care documents hypertension, starts treatment, initiates lifestyle modifications. David is now anchored into the healthcare system. His cardiovascular risk is identified and managed before it becomes a crisis.
outcome_trajectory.missed_diagnosis: If the elevated BP is noted but not explored or followed up on, David goes home thinking his ankle is the only thing that matters. He doesn't establish primary care. His hypertension remains undiagnosed and unmanaged. In two years, he has a stroke or MI that he could have prevented.
outcome_trajectory.resident_catches_it_unsupervised: Resident notes the BP, recognizes it as elevated for an acute pain setting but also elevated in general, asks a few more questions, and makes sure David has a primary care referral and discharge instructions that emphasize getting BP monitored soon. Clean catch.
outcome_trajectory.resident_misses_it_unsupervised: Resident treats the ankle, closes the laceration, documents 'BP elevated in setting of acute pain — likely pain response' and discharges with ankle care. No hypertension workup. No referral. Patient goes home.
outcome_trajectory.follow_up_hook: If the resident does this well, David is now a patient in the Riverside system with a newly diagnosed chronic condition that will require ongoing management. He might come back in for his primary care follow-up in a few weeks. If this went well in the ED, it was because someone took two extra minutes to ask the right questions and make the right referral.

systemic_flags.shift_case_connection: Both this case and the Margaret Chen case feature uninsured/underinsured patients with gaps in primary care access using the ED as their entry point into the healthcare system. David's case is the preventive miss; Margaret's case is the acute miss. Together they illustrate how Riverside General functions as the community's de facto primary care provider.
systemic_flags.future_seed: If David is managed well in this ED visit, he becomes a 'success story' of the healthcare system working as intended — catching someone before they break. If he's missed, he becomes a patient who comes back in a crisis state in six months or a year.
